retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Bone Defect Morphology
retrieved_docs.content: Shallow vertical bone defects range from uneven crestal bone to defects of approximately 3 mm deep, easily recognized on radiographs. [11] A defect is considered deep if it is greater than 3 mm. Three-wall defects are typically found in posterior areas with wide interproximal bone consisting of a buccal, lingual, and interproximal wall. Three-wall defects may also be found at buccal or lingual furcations facing a bone shelf or exostosis. As the mesiodistal volume of the interproximal bone decreases, 3-wall defects may merge and form 2-wall defects called interproximal craters, where only the buccal and lingual walls remain. A 2-wall defect with a buccal and interproximal wall may develop adjacent to the entrance of the interproximal furcation of maxillary molars.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Dental Occlusal Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The following 5 surfaces of the crown of a tooth allow for discussion of occlusal contacts: [8] Facial: oriented toward the lips or teeth and referred to as labial for incisors/canines or buccal for premolars/molars Palatal/Lingual: oriented towards the palate for the maxilla and the tongue for the mandible Mesial: oriented toward the midline Distal: oriented away from the midline Occlusal: oriented toward the upper or lower jaw depending on the mandible versus the maxilla

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Odontogenesis is the formation of teeth. The deciduous or baby teeth develop at 6 to 8 weeks gestation, whereas succedaneous teeth develop at 20 weeks. The ectoderm forms the dental enamel, while the ectomesenchyme (neural crest-derived) forms the dentin, cementum, periodontal ligament, and pulp. Placodes represent invaginations of primary dental lamina within the mandibular and maxillary arches beginning the process of tooth formation. Tooth buds form from the placodes, and the tooth buds develop into teeth. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dens invaginatus has no gender predilection and a prevalence between 0.3 to 10%, depending on the population studied. [9] This condition is mainly identified on the maxillary lateral incisors, followed by the maxillary central incisors. It is very rarely discovered in any other teeth. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: First Molars – The maxillary first molars (tooth B, I) morphologically resemble both a molar and a premolar. Its occlusal surface consists of the mesiobuccal, distolingual, mesiolingual, and distobuccal cusps. The largest cusp is the mesiolingual cusp, and it also represents the sharpest cusp. The distolingual cusp is small and rounded in morphology. The buccal surface is smooth, and the three roots are long and widely spread. Of note, the primary 1st molar has a less pronounced mesiobuccal cusp when compared to the permanent 1st molar.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth affected by fusion appear histologically normal, though the extent and location of fusion influence structural variations. Depending on the developmental stage at which fusion occurs, pulpal chambers, roots, or crowns may be fused or duplicated. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Dens evaginatus, or a “talon cusp,” presents as an outcropped growth of normal tooth structure, commonly affecting the lingual aspect of an anterior maxillary tooth. Talon cusps have been classified into three categories with distinct clinical presentations. [17] Type 1 talon cusps are a morphologically well-differentiated additional cusp that extends half the distance from the cementoenamel junction to the incisal edge. Type 2 talon cusps, known as a “semi-talon,” measure at least one millimeter and extend half the distance from the cementoenamel junction to the incisal edge. Type 2 talon cusps often blend with the surface of the crown. Type 3 talon cusps, or “trace talons,” are enlarged or prominent cingula that can be conical, bifid, or tubercle.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Traditional technique complications
retrieved_docs.content: Craniocervical perforation may develop when the convenience extension is left unfinished before the bur shaft enters the canal space. [63] [61] [62] Pulp debris left behind during preparation may also discolor the crown, especially when the access cavity lacks proper incisal extension and lies too close to the gingival margin. [63] [62] [40] Misjudgment of the tooth's lingual-axial or mesial-axial inclination can result in abrasion of the labial or distal wall during instrumentation. [63] [61] [62] Failure to complete the convenience extension can create a ledge at the apical-labial curve, where the instrument shaft rests on the shoulder or cavity edge instead of advancing smoothly into the canal. [63] [61] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary and Mandibular Dental Arch
retrieved_docs.content: Adult dentition consists of 2 central incisors, 2 lateral incisors, 2 canines, 2 first premolars, 2-second premolars, 2 first molars, 2-second molars, and 2 third molars, both in the maxilla and mandible. The teeth are secured within the alveolar ridges of the maxilla and mandible. Dentition, whether native or restored, is integral to masticatory function. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, the mandibular third molars typically have two shorter, often parallel, mesial, and distal roots with a more pronounced taper than the first or second mandibular molars. [8] Like the maxillary third molar, the mandibular third molar can have significant morphological variation. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Molars – The molars are the largest of the primary teeth and provide significant function in mastication. The primary teeth molars have the same number of roots as the permanent molars. The crowns of the primary molars are more bulbous in morphology, and the roots are more divergent than those of permanent molars.  The buccal, lingual, mesial, and distal surfaces of the maxillary molars are all convex. The occlusal surface is rectangular.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Bone Defect Morphology
retrieved_docs.content: One-wall defects are typically found in areas with narrow interproximal bone, such as closely spaced maxillary molars with furcation involvement or anterior teeth like mandibular incisors. In some cases, localized periodontal bone loss may obliterate the interproximal bone, especially in cases of root proximity, resulting in a localized bone defect with no walls.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.
